title: Do Firms Proactively Build Internal Markets for Redeployment? Evidence from a Natural Experiment
abstract: The topic of related diversification is of fundamental interest to corporate strategy research. However, the understanding of how the potential for resource redeployment might encourage related diversification is limited. This paper explores the impact of the 2006 German real estate transfer tax (RETT) reform, which led to higher RETT rates but retained the exemption from RETT for corporate restructurings. We propose that firms might have reacted strategically to the threat of higher transfer taxes by combining related businesses into a portfolio, providing an efficient internal market for reallocating property assets in the event of business-specific shocks. Consistent with a dynamic perspective on relatedness, difference-in-differences analyses of the European retail market show that the probability of related diversification increased significantly for firms that were affected by the reform, relative to control firms. This finding contributes to the literature that links firm boundary choices to intertemporal economies of scope and offers new insights into the real effects of transfer tax policies.

author.Timo Sohl: UPF Barcelona School of Management, Pompeu Fabra University, 08008 Barcelona, Spain
author.Timothy B. Folta: School of Business, University of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut 06269

article.1. Introduction: Why do multibusiness firms exist given the advantages to focus and the costs associated with corporate headquarters? Rooted inAnsoff (1957),Penrose (1959), andChandler (1962)and reinvigorated byHelfat and Eisenhardt (2004), one explanation gaining credence is that business portfolios enable firms to more flexibly pursue growth opportunities if their internal markets for nonfinancial resources are more efficient than external markets.Lieberman et al. (2017)predict that firms might proactively expand their scope to benefit from greater flexibility. Unfortunately, this prediction remains uninvestigated, largely because it is challenging to ascertain why firms diversify. In this paper, this prediction is tested through a natural experiment where firms change scope in response to expected increases in market frictions. Our inquiry into whether firms intentionally expand horizontal scope to benefit from resource redeployability (i.e., the option to withdraw resources from one business and transfer them to another one) is most closely related to two papers—one theoretical and one empirical.Lieberman et al. (2017)build onDixit (1989)andHelfat and Eisenhardt (2004)to explain that market entry should be more likely for firms having resources that might be switched between current and new markets with low redeployment costs because redeployment potential reduces investment irreversibility and thereby, lowers entry thresholds (i.e., the expected performance required to induce entry). This prediction for scope expansion is unique from prior work emphasizing redeployment potential across an existing portfolio. Their emphasis on diversification into related markets derives from the belief that this is where redeployment costs are generally low because of the limited adjustments required to ready nonfinancial resources for new uses. This explanation for related diversification contrasts with explanations centered on the pursuit of synergy (i.e., intratemporal economies of scope). The natural experimental setting used in our study enables a focus on the redeployability explanation for related diversification. The second paper on which we build is byBelenzon and Tsolmon (2016), who show that businesses in geographies with greater labor market frictions are more likely to be affiliated with a business group to benefit from internal labor markets. Rather than examining how broadly diversified business groups surmount labor market frictions, we consider how related diversified firms overcome real estate frictions burdening market exchange of retail stores. Many types of frictions may inhibit market efficiency. In our empirical context of European retail chains, property transfer taxes, assessed when property changes ownership, may constitute up to 12.5% of property value. So, it should be no surprise that the prospect for higher property transfer taxes reduces market entry because they significantly increase the irreversibility of investment in retail stores (i.e., firms cannot costlessly adjust their stock of property assets in response to either positive or negative shocks to expansion opportunities at one business).1We consider that a different result might be obtained if market entry helps firms develop internal markets for property, and firms can transfer property assets between portfolio businesses with little adjustment (i.e., at low cost). Although several factors may drive internal redeployment costs, consistent with prior work, it is assumed that portfolio relatedness lowers redeployment costs (e.g.,Helfat and Eisenhardt 2004,Sakhartov and Folta 2014,Lieberman et al. 2017,Sohl and Folta 2021). A key prediction is that firms become relatedly diversified to create an internal market for property, enabling them greater flexibility to take better advantage of growth opportunities or limit exposure to a business’s poor prospects. Greater flexibility is obtained because the use of internal markets avoids property transfer tax payments, reducing investment irreversibility. A natural experiment is used to test these ideas. In particular, the 2006 German real estate transfer tax (RETT) reform introduces a source of exogenous variation in the threat of higher transfer tax exposure, enabling a causal interpretation of how horizontal boundary choices are affected by expectations about increased market frictions. The reform empowered the 16 German federal states to set the RETT rate autonomously from 2007 onward while retaining the exemption from RETT for intragroup transactions in the process of corporate restructuring. The reform led to a substantial increase in the transfer tax rate across most German federal states around four years after the passage of the reform (see Table A1 in the Online Appendix). The focus of this paper is on firm responses to thethreatof higher transfer taxes; it suggests that the outcome variable of interest—related diversification—enables an option to redeploy property assets at relatively low cost if transfer taxes increase in the future. The paper also clarifies that this benefit should accrue most robustly when company real estate portfolios contain larger proportions of owned property. The retail market in the European Union (EU) was selected as the empirical context because real estate (i.e., retail stores) is a key asset in retailing and flexibility in adjusting the stock of real estate is of strategic importance for chain organizations. Difference-in-differences (DD) analyses of the 2006 German RETT reform provide causal evidence supportive of our prediction. The probability of related diversification increased by nine percentage points for treated firms in Germany in the years after the 2006 German RETT reform, relative to control firms in other EU countries. Several pieces of evidence give credence to the idea that related diversification is driven by a pursuit to obtain a potential for resource redeployment. For example, the treatment effect on related diversification is attenuated if firms operate in lease-intensive industry segments, supportive of the view that compared with owning, leasing might not provide the same level of discretion to redeploy and impetus to diversify before proposed transfer tax increases are implemented. The treatment has the opposite effect on unrelated diversification. Its effect on related diversification is accentuated when firms have larger real estate holdings, presumably because they might benefit from larger internal markets. The treatment also decreases market values but less so for related diversified firms. Although our natural experiment is specific to Germany, analysis reveals that the results generalize to other countries; increases in property transfer taxes raise the probability of related diversification across Europe, a finding unlikely to be explained by traditional arguments around intratemporal economies or “synergy.” Finally, anecdotes clarify that store redeployment occurs within firms that became relatedly diversified in Germany after 2006. This paper contributes to two streams of literature. First, it extends an emerging stream explaining how redeployment flexibility might create value in an existing portfolio. It does so by investigating if firms diversify to generate redeployment flexibility. To illuminate the dynamic benefits of relatedness, we examine how exogenous variation in expectations about property transfer tax rates affects firm scope decisions, providing new insights into the evolution of horizontal firm boundaries. We also provide the first large-sample evidence around recent theoretical developments emphasizing the influence of redeployment potential on the dynamics of corporate diversification (e.g.,Helfat and Eisenhardt 2004,Lieberman et al. 2017,Sakhartov 2017). A second contribution is in challenging the traditional view that market frictions, such as in the form of property transfer taxes, necessarily have a deleterious effect on entry decisions (e.g.,Dachis et al. 2012,Lieser and Groh 2014,Devaney et al. 2017,Best and Kleven 2018). Our study considers the paradox that an expected increase in property transfer tax rates might encourage entry by established firms into related businesses. By exploiting variation generated by a sharp change in transfer tax policy as a natural experiment, results show that under certain conditions, transfer taxes might increase firm scope rather than decrease it.
article.2. Background and Related Literature: Several bodies of literature bear upon the question of whether diversification is influenced by property transfer taxes. These literatures speak to how property transfer taxes increase investment frictions and how a firm’s internal resource markets might provide a substitute for external market transactions. 2.1. Property Transfer Tax and Corporate Reorganization Property transfer tax is a tax on real estate transactions with the selling price of the property as taxable base. Such taxes are common and raise substantial amounts of revenue in many countries (Best and Kleven 2018). The tax is determined by multiplying the property transaction price by the tax rate. By driving a wedge between the seller and buyer price, property transfer tax reduces asset values because taxes are capitalized into property prices, increasing capital costs to investors (Matheson 2012). Because property transfer taxes are imposed on each transaction, the volume of transactions decreases, which increases the irreversibility of property ownership.Dixit (1989)shows that greater investment irreversibility should make firms slower to enter and slower to exit in general. The literature on financial economics clarifies that transfer taxes are a major barrier to entry and investment, resulting in fewer transactions in the commercial and residential real estate market (e.g.,Dachis et al. 2012,Devaney et al. 2017,Best and Kleven 2018). Indeed, it has been shown that firms extend the holding period of real estate properties in higher transfer tax environments—also known as the lock-in effect (Frenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus 2018). In recognition of a “fiscal unity” between a parent company and its business units or subsidiaries, most countries have laws for tax-free reorganizations, including the exemption of intragroup transfers of property assets from the payment of transfer tax (e.g.,Vanistendael 1998). The objective of granting corporate reorganizations a tax-neutral treatment is that governments want to promote the continuity of firms and shareholder interests in the country. That is, the continuity of control within national boundaries is critical for governments in providing exemptions from transfer tax payments in the process of corporate reorganization (e.g.,Jiménez-Valladolid de L’Hotellerie-Fallois 2014,PwC 2018). 2.2. Potential for Resource Redeployment A recent spate of corporate strategy research emphasizes how internal markets for nonfinancial resources might substantially affect a firm’s scope decisions (e.g.,Anand and Singh 1997,Helfat and Eisenhardt 2004,Levinthal and Wu 2010,Wu 2013,Sakhartov and Folta 2014,Lieberman et al. 2017,Sakhartov 2017,Santamaria 2022). It describes how the connection between relatedness and intertemporal economies of scope is unique from existing theories of corporate advantage (Folta et al. 2016), such as whatStein (2003)labels more-money effects (Lewellen 1971) and smarter-money effects (Stein 1997) in conglomerates.Levinthal and Wu (2010)emphasize that resource redeployment is pertinent for nonscale-free resources—such as physical resources like real estate—because they have capacity constraints, creating opportunity costs that require managers to choose how they should be allocated.2 By focusing on the comparative efficiency of alternative governance structures, such as markets and organizational hierarchy (Williamson 1987), resource redeployment decisions are linked to transaction costs. Several empirical studies show that redeployment benefits are fundamentally related to the presence of market frictions (e.g.,Belenzon and Tsolmon 2016,Dickler and Folta 2020,Giarratana and Santaló 2020,Sohl and Folta 2021). In the absence of market frictions, when the price system reflects the full value of resources correctly accounting for opportunity costs, there would be no advantage of having the potential to internally redeploy resources. Even if market exchange of nonfinancial resources is costly,Helfat and Eisenhardt (2004)clarify that a firm can only profit from intertemporal economies of scope if internal redeployment costs do not offset the benefits of intertemporal resource transfer between businesses. The most closely related research is that ofLieberman et al. (2017), who predict that the potential for resource redeployment increases the probability that firms will become related diversifiers. In their model, firms target related markets for entry because of lower sunk costs associated with such entries because resources can be redeployed relatively inexpensively to cope with changes in opportunities across businesses. Building onDixit (1989), they reason that firms are encouraged to enter related opportunities at lower expected profits (than unrelated opportunities) because of greater reversibility of investment. In this sense, having the potential for resource redeployment is equivalent to a switching real option that lowers the expected performance required to induce entry. 2.3. Diversifying to Benefit from Internal Resource Markets One expectation emanating from the literature is that holding all else equal, increases in external market frictions should make the prospect for internal markets more attractive and inspire related diversification to benefit from such markets. However, to our knowledge, no prior empirical work has examined the prediction that firms proactively pursue a related diversification strategy to establish internal markets where nonfinancial resources can be redeployed. Indeed, this is the primary focus of our empirical effort, which is aided by a regulatory change in German real estate transfer tax affecting the market exchange costs for real estate—a firm-flexible (tradable) and nonscale-free resource. We believe that this shock, described in the next section, will help illuminate whether redeployment potential might influence related diversification. A secondary focus is on clarifying that the resources at the heart of the redeployment decision need not be owned. Although ownership provides full discretion to redeploy, leasing might also facilitate redeployment, even if it does not offer the same level of discretion. Although leasing is a contractual governance structure, it may provide many of the same flexibility benefits for redeployment, provided that the lessee obtains contractual stipulations enabling discretion to redeploy.
article.3. The 2006 German Real Estate Transfer: Real estate transfer taxes are a common and important source of government revenues in many Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries (Andrews et al. 2011). There is significant variation in tax rates across Europe, with Belgium imposing one of the highest tax rates on real estate transactions, whereas almost half of European member states have transfer tax rates below 5%. The German real estate transfer tax rate was set nationwide at 3.5% (on the sales price) between 1997 and 2006. In 2006, the RETT reform was passed by the German Parliament and dictated that each of the 16 German federal states could autonomously set the RETT rate after 2006.3The reform ultimately led to a substantial increase in the RETT rate for 14 of 16 federal states in Germany by 2017 (see Table A1 in the Online Appendix). The average rate by 2017 was 5.4%. By 2011, the real estate transfer tax amounted to nearly 52% of the average property transaction costs (Fritzsche and Vandrei 2019). Additional details of this legislation and the corresponding increases are worth elaborating. First, tax increases were not immediate. One state (i.e., Berlin) raised RETT from its base rate of 3.5% as early as 2007, one (i.e., Hamburg) raised RETT in 2009, one raised RETT in 2010, seven raised RETT in 2011, and six raised RETT in 2012. Ten states raised the RETT rate multiple times by 2017, including Berlin, which raised a second time in 2012 and a third time in 2014. In total, across the 16 states, there were 27 rate increases and no decreases between 2007 and 2017. So, the prospect of increased taxes was a real threat among retail firms throughout that 11-year postreform period, allowing for an extended time window to act before (further) increases raised the sunk entry cost. Second, the RETT reform created incentives for German states to increase tax rates to better cope with the German federation system of fiscal equalization, where financial resources are redistributed across states to mitigate differences in fiscal capacity and expenditures (Buettner and Krause 2021). Because of the way fiscal capacity is defined by the German federation, higher tax rates have an adverse effect on fiscal capacity, which reflects the deadweight loss from taxation. Hence, a state that raises its tax rate receives more rather than fewer equalization transfers.4The incentives to increase property tax rates were boosted further with a 2009 reform establishing state borrowing limits and imposing balanced budgets, effective 2020 (Fritzsche and Vandrei 2019). The RETT offered states an important means of achieving some revenue autonomy, withChristofzik et al. (2020)reporting that in 2019, RETT taxes constituted 61.1% of tax revenues captured at the state level, and it is the only tax where states can set their own tax rate. Third, the anticipation of tax increases and the preannouncement of such increases led to a surge of property purchases just prior to implementation of tax increases. Within each state, changes to Germany’s RETT rates are typically announced several months in advance because they must be approved by state parliaments.Christofzik et al. (2020)show that most tax increases were foreseeable (in press announcements) up to three quarters prior to their implementation.Fritzsche and Vandrei (2019)find that media citations of the tax reform are particularly high during the time that tax changes are announced or discussed by state parliaments and much less active around the actual date of tax increase. Accordingly, the timing of the tax changes is in most cases largely anticipated, with 46% more residential housing transactions in the months prior to a tax increase. Similar anticipatory effects were found in housing markets with changes to RETT in France (Poulhès 2020), the United Kingdom (Best and Kleven 2018), and Washington, DC (Slemrod et al. 2017). Only one study examines the anticipatory effects in commercial real estate markets, which are the focus of this paper, and finds some evidence that they exist one year prior to changes in German RETT rates (Frenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus 2018). Overall, the evidence suggests that many market participants accelerate planned property purchases to take advantage of the lower tax rate in the period between announced and implemented transfer tax increases. Fourth, subsequent to implementation of tax increases, property transaction volume dropped precipitously. Indeed, it is the lower volume of transactions after implementation of property taxes that describes the “lock-in” effect of transfer taxes because of how reduced liquidity increases sunk costs of property investment.Frenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus (2018)find that commercial property transactions in Germany drop with increases in property transfer taxes, whereasLieser and Groh (2014)find the same effect in commercial real estate investments in 47 countries.5An increase in the German RETT by 1% reduced single-family home transactions by 6% (Fritzsche and Vandrei 2019), whereasFrenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus (2018)find that commercial property transaction volume declines by almost 12% with a 1.15% increase in taxes. The lock-in effect has also been observed with property tax increases in Toronto (Dachis et al. 2012); Washington, DC (Slemrod et al. 2017); Australia (Davidoff and Leigh 2013); and France (Poulhès 2020). Fifth, subsequent to implementation of tax increases, it was not obvious whether leasing became relatively more attractive than purchasing. Prior research on the German RETT policy confirms that property values decline for commercial (Frenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus 2018) and residential (e.g.,Fritzsche and Vandrei 2019,Dolls et al. 2021,Koetter et al. 2022) properties when transfer taxes are implemented.6The negative price reaction with property transfer tax increases is also robustly confirmed in locations outside of Germany (e.g.,Dachis et al. 2012,Davidoff and Leigh 2013,Besley et al. 2014,Best and Kleven 2018). The impact on the average property price reflects the expectation that a given property will be subject to the tax each time it is sold, and thus, the expected incidence of the tax increase is capitalized into property prices. There is less evidence around the impact of RETT on lease rates. Although lessees are not subject to the property tax payment, lessors are. This suggests that landlords may compensate for their loss in investment (because of greater irreversibility) by increasing the burden on tenants (Frenzel Baudisch and Dresselhaus 2018,Lai and Milcheva 2023,Schneider and Wrede 2023).7Higher rental rates should not be surprising because rent needs to cover all costs and yield a positive return for real estate lessors to be competitive among capital investments (Poterba 1984).Schneider and Wrede (2023)report that higher German real estate transfer taxes do not affect the probability of residential moves from renting to owning.Koetter et al. (2022)find that higher RETT rates lead to higher residential rental prices in urban areas of Germany but lower rent prices in rural areas. Studies of RETT changes in the United Kingdom confirm higher rental prices after transaction tax increases (Lai and Milcheva 2023). Given that property values and liquidity tend to decline and that lease rates tend to increase after transfer tax increases, it is ambiguous, ex ante, how the relative attractiveness of owning versus leasing is altered with transfer tax increases.8 Sixth, the reform did not affect the tax neutrality of real estate transactions within firms in the process of corporate restructuring. In particular,Section 6a of the German RETT Act provides for intragroup restructuring transactions, an exemption from RETT payments. An important condition for the exemption to apply is that the restructuring transaction must involve one “controlling entity” (corporate parent) and one or more “controlled entities” (business units or subsidiaries). It also must involve a direct or indirect shareholding of at least 95% between the RETT group members for the five years immediately before and after the transaction (“prior holding” and “subsequent holding” periods). This five-year stipulation is consequential in that if firms competing in Germany expected that RETT would increase, they would benefit more by diversifying quickly to gain flexibility through an internal market for property. This study examines whether firms exposed to the German RETT reform and therefore, thethreatof higher transfer taxes were encouraged to become related diversifiers because of the higher relative benefits of internal resource markets. Having related businesses enables multibusiness firms to shift resources to businesses undergoing positive shocks and/or away from businesses undergoing negative shocks. Potential shocks to investment opportunities at one business can be either endogenous (caused by the firm) or exogenous (caused by factors outside of the control of the firm). It may be that one of a firm’s businesses will need capacity in property assets to fuel its growth, and internal redeployment is the more efficient alternative if internal redeployment costs are less than the transaction costs associated with the market. Presumably, even if a business is not performing poorly, there may be advantages of redeploying (part of) its property assets that have opportunity costs inside the firm to better-performing portfolio businesses. So, by combining related businesses in a portfolio, firms have the right, but not the obligation, to reallocate property assets at relatively low cost if there are inducements (i.e., performance differences between portfolio businesses) to do so. Firms can exercise this option in the future if business-specific shocks create positive inducements net of internal redeployment costs, which exceed the value that can be obtained using external market transactions. The theory described suggests that the threat of higher transfer taxes may inspire related diversification to enable property redeployment and avoid sunk costs tied to higher taxes on future external transactions. Some elaboration is necessary given that, in retail, leasing is a common alternative to owning, and company real estate portfolios almost always contain some mixture of owned and leased real estate. These governance modes may differ with regard to (a) the discretion they allow to redeploy and (b) the impetus for diversifying in anticipation of transfer tax increases. Complete discretion to redeploy should be available through property ownership (as described inSection 2.2), so the theory should apply with strongest force when ownership dominates a firm’s real estate portfolio. For several reasons, leasing should also enable discretion to redeploy between leased and owned buildings or among leased buildings, even if it is at some reduced level relative to ownership. Redeployment discretion might be constrained or devalued if contracts allow for early termination by the landlord, allow for rent revisions, disallow rights of renewal, or disallow rights to sublease internally (i.e., between portfolio businesses). The German Civil Code disallows early lease termination by the landlord (other than a tenant breach), and the tenant may sublease with landlord consent (and can only be refused for good cause), although leases to the corporate parent (rather than one of its businesses) do not require subleasing to allow redeployment. Other leasing stipulations that may influence redeployment discretion might be determined by firm-specific negotiating power (Smith and Wakeman 1985), such as rights of renewal and rent revision.9Greater discretion to redeploy via ownership should translate to greater flexibility to dynamically adapt property to portfolio businesses with better opportunities, and the value of such flexibility may offset any static efficiency advantage of leasing at a single point in time. Given that ownership may provide more discretion than leasing to redeploy and that transfer taxes have a direct bearing on the irreversibility of investment in owned property, we expect firms having property portfolios with larger proportions of owned property to be more likely to undertake related diversification prior to property transfer tax increases. The impetus to diversify through property ownership should be especially pronounced if transfer taxes are expected to increase, so the diversifier can enable an internal property market without incurring the additional tax upon property acquisition. The impetus to diversify through leasing may be unaffected by expectations around transfer tax increases because it is unclear whether and when lease rates increase to capitalize future taxes on property transactions. However, because nearly all retail diversification contains some combination of owned and leased property, there should be some impetus for diversification prior to tax increases, with stronger impetus when the diversification involves a higher proportion of owned buildings. Although the focus of this paper is on related diversification, additional analysis reported inSection 6.4also explores whether and how the German RETT reform affected the propensity for German retail firms to lease property. Although firms may proactively diversify to create internal markets for property, it is worth elaborating available alternatives to related diversification beyond leasing in response to such a shock. The prospect of a transfer tax increase may inspire firms (a) seeking synergy to diversify sooner or (b) to diversify outside Germany. We also compare whether proposed transfer tax increases asymmetrically affect related versus unrelated diversification. It is elaborated inSection 4how unrelated diversifiers in our context lack potential to redeploy because it is extraordinarily costly to transform retail stores if portfolio chains use different store formats. The empirical analysis considers each alternative.
article.4. Empirical Design: 4.1. Empirical Context The EU retail market is used to test whether retail firms might have reacted strategically to the threat of higher transfer taxes by combining related businesses into a portfolio. The potential for redeployment of property assets is pertinent because many retail firms own multiple businesses or chains, whereas others are single-chain firms. The context is also attractive for testing the theory because it allows for a focus on the potential for redeployment of retail stores—a real asset of strategic and financial importance to multiunit organizations in many service industries, such as retail, hotel, restaurant, retail banking, and car rental (Garvin and Levesque 2008). Because redeployment of real estate is limited to portfolio businesses within a country, a focus on redeployment costs provides an important boundary condition on the potential for resource redeployment.10Provided that redeployment can be made to a sibling chain within a country, firms still face redeployment costs (e.g., costs of changing a store’s banner, interior design, and product assortment) to ready the real estate for use in an alternative chain. Although several factors may drive redeployment costs, lower redeployment costs should be obtained for retail stores in a country when there are related sibling chains in the country. Business relatedness can be captured at the detailed submarket level within the retail industry by whether portfolio chains use the same store format. Store formats are commonly used in retailing to group similar chains based on physical properties, such as store size, location, and function (e.g.,Gonzales-Benito et al. 2005,Cleeren et al. 2010,Corstjens and Lal 2012); they differentially characterize many different types of retail outlets, including but not limited to convenience stores, supermarkets, hypermarkets, department stores, and warehouse clubs. Consider, for example, the two Spain-based multichain clothing retailers “El Corte Inglés” and “Inditex.” El Corte Inglés operates multiple retail chains across store formats, such as the chains El Corte Inglés (department store format), Sfera (clothing store format), Opencor (convenience store format), Supercor (supermarket format), and Hipercor (hypermarket format). In contrast, Inditex operates eight chains (e.g., Zara, Berksha, Ohsho, and Pull & Bear) in the same clothing store format. Although both parent retailers likely benefit from the potential for resource sharing across retail chains (i.e.,intratemporaleconomies of scope), Inditex may have an additional advantage: the option to switch retail stores at low cost between portfolio chains (i.e.,intertemporaleconomies of scope). The refined measurement of relatedness at the format level facilitates the study of redeployment costs of retail stores, which likely increase rapidly if businesses are less related, suggesting that redeployment potential should be influenced by whether portfolio chains adopt the same store format. For example, it would be economically unreasonable to transform a large hypermarket store into a small convenience store. In contrast, converting a department store from one department store chain to another will be economically feasible and more achievable because it does not require changes to a completely different store format, even if it might require adjustments in the form of signage and promotion. This is what Federated Department Stores did in 2001 when it closed its struggling Stern’s department store chain in the United States and converted nearly 80% of the stores to its faster-growing Macy’s and Bloomingdale’s department store chains (Kaufman 2001). It follows that redeployment costs are relatively low if stores are converted for use by another chain in the same store format and that they should be excessively high if the alternative chain operates in a different store format. To capture these differences in redeployment potential, “same-format diversification” is defined as related diversification, and “different-format diversification” is defined as unrelated diversification. So, if El Corte Inglés was to move from one to two chains in a country, it would represent unrelated diversification, whereas a similar move by Inditex would represent related diversification. Anecdotal evidence suggests that corporate parents recognize the potential for value creation by redeploying retail stores, both purchased and leased units, to their more productive chains if portfolio chains are related. During the coronavirus disease 2019 pandemic, for example, parent retailer Wesfarmers redeployed in Australia 92 stores from its struggling chain Target to its same-format sister chain Kmart while closing about 75 stores.11As a business reporter explained, Target has been “struggling for some time, really muscled out by its own stronger sister company, Kmart” (Wood 2020). Similarly, Foot Locker redeployed during the pandemic 18 stores of its Footaction chain to its better-performing same-format chains, such as Foot Locker and Champs Sports, while closing 32 Footaction stores (Foot Locker 2021). Chairman and chief executive officer (CEO) Johnson said on a call with analysts that “the company tested changing some Footaction stores into Foot Lockers and those locations became more productive as a result of the shift, which helped inform the decision to convert many of those stores” (Salpini 2021). In 2020, Foot Locker also decided to shut down its struggling Runners Point chain by redeploying several stores into other portfolio chains. For example, 72 Runners Point stores were closed in Germany, many of which were converted into Foot Locker stores (HahnAG 2021). Another example is Delhaize Group, which redeployed in the United States 42 stores from its poorly performing Blooms chain to its better-performing Food Lion chain—both operating in the supermarket format while shutting down 7 stores.12In Australia, the United States, and Germany, corporate reorganizations are exempt from property transfer taxes if the ultimate ownership of the property remains the same (e.g.,Gruidl and Kong 2012,Parkyn and Courtney 2016). Although anecdotal evidence suggests that parent retailers exploit internal markets for real property (stores), no systematic research examines if the creation of such internal markets might be inspired by the dynamic benefits of relatedness arising from redeployability. 4.2. Data Unbalanced panel data of 955 publicly and privately held physical store-based retail firms in 27 EU countries during the eight-year window from 2003 to 2010 (three years before and four years after the 2006 German RETT reform) were obtained from Edge Retail Insight (formerly Planet Retail), totaling 6,083 firm-country-year observations.13The data include retailers active in different industry segments, such as grocery, clothing and footwear, health and beauty, consumer electronics, leisure and entertainment, home and garden, and food service (i.e., restaurants and bars). An advantage of these data relative to Compustat Segment data, which have been criticized because a firm’s segments may not adequately reflect its actual business lines (e.g.,Villalonga 2004), is that it provides a setting that allows for a refined measurement of business lines at the chain level. Besides providing information on retail firms and their business lines (chains) in a given country and year, the database also provides information on the store format and the number of stores of each chain. In particular, each chain operates multiple stores under the same banner (i.e., brand name) in a given store format, and none of the sampled chains changed their store format during the period studied. Analysis is at the firm-country-year level. In the case of the France-based Carrefour Group, for example, the firm-country level corresponds to country divisions of Carrefour, such as Carrefour France and Carrefour Spain. Across these firm-country observations, the data illuminate whether Carrefour operates multiple chains (e.g., Carrefour City, Carrefour Express, Proxi, Carrefour Market, and Carrefour Hypermarket) and the store formats specific to the chains (e.g., convenience store, supermarket, and hypermarket).14 Table 1provides summary statistics for the 6,083 firm-country-year observations. As shown inTable 1, 17.1% of observations are firms that are related diversified (i.e., operating at least two chains with the same format in a given country and year).15Additionally, 6.5% of observations are 59 firms active in Germany (i.e., the treated group), meaning that 93.5% are firms active in the other 26 countries that were part of the EU during the sample period (i.e., the control group). Also, 54.9% of the observations occur after the 2006 reform. Measurement Framework Difference-in-differences analyses exploit the 2006 German real estate transfer tax reform to identify how expected changes in market frictions affect the probability of a firm undertaking related diversification in a country. A linear probability regression model is used, where the dependent variableRelated diversified firmequals one if retail firmioperates (at least) two same-format chains in countrycat yeartand zero otherwise.16A panel fixed effects (FEs) model is used with the following regression specification: Observations are treated ifGerman firms postreformequals one, meaning that a firm is active in Germany after the reform between 2007 and 2010;German firms postreformequals zero if a firm is active outside of Germany or in Germany prior to 2007.17So,δis the coefficient of interest, capturing how the RETT reform influences the dependent variable, which should be a proxy for whether a firm proactively builds an internal market to enable the potential for real estate redeployment. The specification also includes sets of firm-country fixed effectsαicand year fixed effectsαt.Controlsictdenotes a vector of observed time-varying control variables defined in Table A2 in the Online Appendix. It aims to control for variation at the level of the firm (i.e., parent public firm, parent product diversification, and parent international diversification), the country (i.e., Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita, GDP per capita growth, inflation rate, internet users, market concentration, tax burden, investment freedom, financial freedom, and monetary freedom), and the firm-country (i.e., size, years since country entry, and firm-country sales/firm sales). Finally,εictis an independent and identically distributed error term. Because the treatment effect is at the country level, standard errors are adjusted for clustering at the country level.Section 5.2considers alternative ways of computing standard errors. The DD estimator identifies a causal effect of the treatment by netting out trends from the control group. It does so by assuming that trends of related diversification in the control group represent trends in what would have happened in the treatment group in the absence of treatment. Analysis provides strong support for the parallel-trends assumption and a causal interpretation of the treatment effect.Figure 1plots the means of the dependent variable over time for the treatment and control groups (the left panel ofFigure 1). It also visualizes the results of the linear-trends model (the right panel ofFigure 1), conditional on country and year effects. The trajectories of related diversification appear parallel for both groups prior to the 2006 German RETT reform but after the reform the lines diverge. The fraction of related diversified firms increased significantly in the treated group after 2006, and this trajectory does not show any tendency toward returning to the levels prior to the 2006 RETT reform. A formal test for parallel trends in the pretreatment period, conditional on country and year effects, does not reject the null hypothesis of parallel trends (F= 0.02;p= 0.89). Prior to running the model specified inEquation (1), lead and lag estimations were generated by interacting a German firm indicator variable (i.e., if the firm was active in Germany = 1) with year indicator variables in relation to the 2006 RETT reform. The dependent variable isRelated diversified firm.Table 2reports the results. The prereform coefficients are insignificant, suggesting that related diversification for German firms is similar to firms in other EU countries. However, the postreform coefficients are significant, suggesting that related diversification is significantly more likely for German firms than for their EU counterparts (Figure A1 in the Online Appendix illustrates the estimated coefficients and their 95% confidence intervals). article.5. Results: 5.1. Difference-in-Differences Estimates Table 3reports the main results, diagnosing the effect of the 2006 German RETT reform on related diversification. Results indicate that the estimated treatment effects are not driven by the choice of control variables. Results across columns (1)–(3) inTable 3show that the probability of related diversification increased significantly for firms in Germany after the 2006 German RETT reform relative to firms in other EU countries. All coefficient estimates are statistically significant at the 1% level. The estimated treatment effect is sizable; it lies between 9.0 and 9.2 percentage points. Conditional on country and year effects, results in column (1) inTable 3indicate that in the years after 2006, the probability of related diversification increased in the treated group by 9.1 percentage points per year, on average, relative to the control group. Conditional on firm-country effects, year effects, and control variables, column (3) inTable 3indicates that a firm in Germany had an 9.0-percentage-point increase in its probability of related diversification following the RETT reform relative to if the firm would not have been exposed to the reform. Overall, the results suggest a causal effect of the 2006 German RETT reform on decisions to pursue a related diversification strategy, consistent with a story around the dynamic benefits of relatedness arising from redeployability. 5.2. Robustness Checks A number of robustness checks assess how confident we should be in results reported inTable 3. All are available upon request. A potential concern is that relatively few German firms moved from not relatedly diversified to relatedly diversified in the postreform period. To assess whether the small number of entry events by German firms after 2006 biases our results, several approaches were used. An initial approach examines whether the results are driven by any single firm by iteratively excluding each of the eight treated firms that became relatedly diversified after 2006 but were not relatedly diversified before the tax reform. This unreported analysis reveals that results are not driven by any single firm. To increase the number of portfolio reconfiguration events, a second approach alters the dependent variable to a firm’s number of related businesses in the given country and year. This raises the number of treated observations to 14 changes in the number of related businesses by firms in Germany after 2006. This change in the dependent variable yields positive and significant (p< 1%) effects: 4.2- to 5.5-percentage-point increases in the probability that a German firm increases its number of same-format chains in any given year following the tax reform relative to control firms in other EU countries. The lower overall treatment effect using this more inclusive dependent variable may also suggest only a marginal benefit to redeployability when having more than two related businesses in a portfolio. A final robustness check, given the small number of German diversifiers, examines patterns of related diversification in response to large increases in transfer tax in other EU countries between 2003 and 2010. This expands the number of initial related entries (i.e., when a firm moves from not relatedly diversified to relatedly diversified) to 26 entries after hikes in transfer taxes. This analysis is presented inSection 6.2and shows that property transfer tax increases raise the probability of related diversification across Europe. These three approaches help dismiss concerns about sample size driving our results. Additional robustness tests were conducted. All these tests produced substantively similar results to those reported inTable 3. First, as an alternative to including country and year fixed effects, theTreated(i.e., firms in Germany) andPost(i.e., years after 2006) variables were included (see Table A5 in the Online Appendix). Second, logit regression models were used instead of linear probability models (see Table A6 in the Online Appendix).18Third, a more rigid DD analysis reported in Table A7 in the Online Appendix compares only observations for treated and control groups from the same parent firm (i.e., all parent retailers having no operations in Germany were dropped from this analysis). Fourth, segment-year joint fixed effects were included in all specifications (columns (1)–(3) inTable 3) to account for shocks during the sample period that were specific to a firm’s industry segment measured at the two-digit Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code level (the core segment was used if a firm operates in multiple segments). Fifth, given the relatively small number of groups in our setting (i.e., 27 EU countries), alternatives for computing standard errors were implemented using (a) clustering at the firm-country level rather than the country level (see Table A8 in the Online Appendix), (b) bias-corrected standard errors with theBell and McCaffrey (2002)degrees-of-freedom adjustment, and (c) the aggregation method proposed byDonald and Lang (2007). Finally, to account for alternative macroeconomic and political factors potentially influencing our results, controls were added using interactions between the treated variable (i.e., firms in Germany) and country-level variables (i.e., GDP per capita, GDP per capita growth, inflation rate, internet users, market concentration, economic freedom indicators, and an employment protection index).19 In addition to the tests reported, a Granger causality test evaluated whether conditional on country and year effects, the treated and control groups changed their strategy of related diversification in anticipation of the 2006 German RETT reform. The null hypothesis of no effect in anticipation of treatment is not rejected (F= 0.96;p= 0.43), suggesting that firms did not change their related diversification behavior in anticipation of the reform. To further mitigate potential concerns that firms with certain characteristics might select countries in anticipation of the 2006 German RETT reform, firms that entered a given country after 2003 were excluded from the sample; results were robust.
article.6. Additional Analyses: The analysis reported gives credence to the causal interpretation that firms implement related diversification prior to likely property transfer tax increases. Although this evidence is consistent with the idea that firms generate an internal real estate market to benefit from redeployability, the next subsections seek to further establish this theoretical interpretation as likely. This is done partly by driving at underlying mechanisms and partly by comparing alternative explanations. 6.1. Related Diversification vs. Unrelated Diversification Analysis thus far shows how treatment affects related diversification. If the pursuit of internal real estate markets drives these effects, we would not expect analysis of unrelated diversification to produce the same effects. Unrelated diversification should be less sensitive to increases in real estate transfer taxes because store redeployment should be more costly across different store formats. The probability of diversification might be expected to decrease if firms do not plan to invoke internal markets because of higher sunk costs tied to real estate transactions after tax increases are implemented. To test the prediction clarified, a dependent variable is created;Unrelated diversified firmequals one if a focal retail firm operates (at least) two different-format chains in a given country and year and equals zero otherwise.Figure 2plots the means of the dependent variable over time and visualizes the results of the linear-trends model. Although the trajectories appear somewhat different between the treated and control groups in the pretreatment period, results of the parallel-trends test do not reject the null hypothesis of parallel trends (F= 2.79;p= 0.11).20Columns (1)–(3) inTable 4report a negative effect of the indicator variable for firms in Germany after 2006 on the probability of unrelated diversification. The negative relation between proposed transfer taxes and unrelated diversification reported contrasts sharply with our results showing increases in related diversification following the tax reform. This result is consistent with a redeployment story.21 Table 4.Results for the Probability of Unrelated Diversification Table 4.Results for the Probability of Unrelated Diversification Notes. Analysis is at the firm-country-year level. Related Diversification Around the EU The analysis was expanded to study whether property transfer tax changes influence related diversification across EU countries. This helps in several ways. First, the number of related entries (i.e., firms moving from not relatedly diversified to relatedly diversified) increases from 8 entries by firms in Germany after 2006 to 26 entries by firms in other EU countries after transfer tax hikes, reducing concerns about small numbers driving results. Second, it provides more generalizable evidence around our phenomenon of interest. Third, it helps dismiss concerns that other (i.e., nonreform) events in Germany around 2006 might explain related diversification. Firms should be more likely to be related diversified in countries with higher property transfer taxes. A variable,Property transfer tax, is measured at the country-year level with the “tax rate on transfer of property” from the European Commission’s housing taxation database.22Descriptive analyses provide some initial insight. Using a median split to distinguish between higher versus lower transfer tax environments, two-sample tests reveal that the proportion of related diversified firms is significantly different at the 1% level in high versus low transfer tax environments (20.1% versus 13.5%, respectively), whereas the proportion of unrelated diversified firms is similar in both tax environments (approximately 29%). To examine whether firms are more likely to build an internal market for real estate in response to noticeable increases in property transfer tax in a given country, an indicator variable was created, whereTransfer tax hikeequals one if a tax increase from the prior to the current year falls in the top 5% of the changes in property transfer tax distribution. We also explore whether noticeable decreases in property transfer tax might influence the probability of related diversification, whereTransfer tax cutequals one if a tax decrease falls in the bottom 5% of the changes in property transfer tax distribution. It is noteworthy that German firms are “control” firms in this analysis. Multivariate regressions are presented in Table A9 in the Online Appendix. Columns (1)–(5) in Table A9 in the Online Appendix report effects on related diversification, showing that all coefficient estimates of theTransfer tax hikevariable are positive and statistically significant at the 5% level. For example, column (5) in Table A9 in the Online Appendix indicates that a hike in a country’s transfer tax rate (i.e., a tax increase of more than 3%) enhances the probability of related diversification by a firm in that country by 1.7 percentage points. Note that these effects are lower than the effects reported inTable 3, but this analysis studies actual tax increases, whereas earlier analysis studied proposed (but not yet enacted) tax increases, where the impetus to diversify should be higher. Results also reveal insignificant effects of theTransfer tax cutvariable, perhaps because the potential for synergy is unaffected by the efficiency of real estate markets, and firms might remain relatedly diversified if transfer taxes decrease to avoid the loss of synergy. Columns (6)–(10) in Table A9 in the Online Appendix show that the probability of unrelated diversification is not significantly affected by changes in transfer tax, consistent with our explanations inSection 6.1. By expanding our analysis beyond Germany, these findings provide additional support of the view that higher market frictions caused by transfer tax enhance the relative advantage of related diversifiers. 6.3. Lease- vs. Ownership-Intense Industry Segments The following analysis explores the possibility that property leasing versus owning may bear upon a firm’s discretion to redeploy and its impetus for diversifying prior to a tax increase. This analysis exploits differences in lease ratios across 10 retail industry segments as illustrated in Table B1 in Online Appendix B, where lease ratio is the ratio of the capitalized operating lease expenditure to the sum of that amount and the amount of property, plant, and equipment (PP&E) (Beatty et al. 2010).23For example, segments consisting of food stores (SIC 54), eating and drinking establishments (SIC 58), general merchandise stores (SIC 53), and apparel and accessory stores (SIC 56) have median lease ratios of 42.8%, 53.6%, 62.9%, and 85.4%, respectively. To examine whether the response to the tax reform is weaker among retail firms in lease-intense industry segments, an indicator variable is created, whereLease-intense segmentequals one if the median lease ratio of a firm’s industry segment exceeds the retail median lease ratio and zero otherwise. Cross-sectional variation in the treatment effect is examined by including interactions between the treatment variable (i.e.,German firms postreform) andLease-intense segment.Table 5reports the results. Columns (1) and (2) inTable 5show that results from the main analysis remain whenLease-intense segmentis included. Columns (3)–(5) inTable 5show negative and significant coefficients for the interaction between theGerman firms postreformandLease-intense segmentvariables, indicating that the effect of the RETT reform on the probability of related diversification is attenuated for firms in lease-intense segments. Coefficient estimates (i.e., column (4) inTable 5) show that following the tax reform, the probability of related diversification increases by 13.0 percentage points for retailers in ownership-intense industry segments, whereas it increases by 3.1 percentage points for retailers in lease-intense industry segments relative to control firms. The difference is statistically significant at the 1% level.24It is noteworthy that the tax reform increased the probability of related diversification for firms in both own-intense and lease-intense segments. Although the results of this analysis fall short of a causal interpretation, they are consistent with the idea that related diversification decisions by firms more oriented toward leasing are less sensitive to changes in transfer tax rates. 6.4. Lease Intensity Following the Tax Reform One might argue, for several reasons, that the German RETT reform increases the relative attractiveness of leasing versus owning. First, higher property transfer taxes increase the sunkness of property ownership. Second, leasing may allow discretion to redeploy while avoiding sunk investment in property.Section 3argued that, ex ante, it is ambiguous how property transfer taxes should affect the relative attractiveness of leasing versus owning, but here, we empirically test whether firm-level lease intensities increased subsequent to the tax reform. Two additional data sets were obtained (see Online Appendix B for details). The first is a sample of all public retail firms in EU countries with lease ratio information included in the Compustat Global database. An advantage of the Compustat Global analysis is that it allows a comparison between treated firms (i.e., firms listed on public exchange in Germany) with control firms (i.e., firms listed on public exchanges in other EU countries). Table B2 in the Online Appendix reports results using this sample andLease ratioas the dependent variable (i.e., the ratio of a firm’s capitalized operating lease payment to the sum of that amount and the amount of PP&E). There is a negative association (p< 1%) between the indicator variable for firms in Germany after 2006 and the lease ratio variable. Column (3) in Table B2 in the Online Appendix indicates that conditional on firm effects, year effects, and control variables, subsequent to the RETT reform a German firm’s lease ratio decreased by 6.9 percentage points, on average, relative to control firms in other EU countries. The second is a proprietary sample from the Corporate Balance Sheets (Ustan) 1987–2019 data set (Becker et al. 2020), which was provided by the Research Data and Service Centre of the Deutsche Bundesbank (for the project number 2023\0026 with on-site data access: GaFo). It includes public and private retail firms in Germany with information on whether company buildings are predominantly leased (one if yes). An advantage of the Bundesbank data is the comprehensiveness of retail firms in Germany (i.e., 1,502 firms). Table B3 in the Online Appendix shows that firm-level lease intensities were not significantly different between the postreform period (2007–2010) and the prereform period (2003–2006). Table B4 in the Online Appendix examines how actual transfer tax changes affect lease intensities. Tax increases are negatively associated with the probability that German retailers predominantly lease buildings (p< 5%). The analysis in this section does not lend support to the view that leasing becomes relatively more attractive with either proposed or implemented property transfer tax increases. 6.5. Synergy Although results are consistent with a redeployment story, it is possible that they may also be explained by the fact that proposed but not yet enacted tax increases inspire earlier entry by firms that were otherwise planning to diversify to take advantage of synergy. Four issues lead us to believe that our related entrants are not pursuing synergy. First,Levinthal and Wu (2010)emphasize that synergy is most practical for scale-free resources, and real estate is a nonscale-free resource. Second, anecdotal evidence reported inSection 6.10illuminates that redeployment of real estate occurred subsequent to related entry by German firms in the postreform period. Third, analysis presented inSection 6.2shows a significant relationship between noticeable property transfer tax increases and related diversification across the EU, irrespective of any proposed increases providing a window to expand before increases accrue. We cannot think of any reason why related diversification to pursue synergy would be positively related to property transfer tax increases. A fourth issue leading us to believe that synergy is not driving the results is elaborated inSection 6.6. 6.6. Company Managed vs. Franchised Stores Assumptions about flexibility to redeploy may depend on whether chain stores are company managed or franchised; redeploying stores between chains might be more feasible and less costly when they are company managed rather than franchisee operated. BecauseHolmstrom and Roberts (1998)argue that restaurant chains often use business-concept franchising, whereas other chain stores, such as supermarkets, clothing stores, and department stores, are typically company managed, an indicator variable was created for restaurant firms to capture a firm’s focus on franchising in the given country.25Table A11 in the Online Appendix shows a negative and significant interaction effect between theGerman firms postreformandRestaurant firmvariables (p< 1%), which would be expected if firms respond less to the threat of higher transfer taxes by becoming relatedly diversified when their business model relies more on franchising. To elaborate, the coefficient estimates in column (4) in Table A11 in the Online Appendix indicate that the probability of related diversification increased by only 0.7 percentage points for restaurant firms, whereas it increased by 10.3 percentage points for nonrestaurant firms. If restaurant firms have less potential for store redeployment but similar potential for synergy (e.g., via knowledge sharing across chains) as compared with other retail firms, this finding provides evidence supportive of the view that redeployability, rather than synergy, inspires related diversification in response to the RETT reform. 6.7. Real Estate Portfolio Size If the proposed effects around redeployability are correct, then firms more likely to pursue related diversification should be those operating more real estate assets. This is because firms should be more likely to use an internal market more extensively if a larger number of stores is subject to potential redeployment.Table 6provides evidence supportive of this view, showing a positive and significant interaction (p< 1%) between theGerman firms postreformvariable and an indicator variable that equals one if a firm-country’s number of stores is above the sample median in the year before the tax reform on the probability of related diversification.26To elaborate, column (4) inTable 6shows that following the tax reform, the probability of related diversification by a firm in Germany increases by 2.7 percentage points if the firm operates few stores, whereas it increases by 10.3 percentage points if the firm operates many stores in the year before the tax reform relative to firms in other EU countries.
