Geographic Diversification It is likely that the prospect for property transfer tax increases in Germany spurred firms to invest in other countries rather than Germany. This does not preclude our explanation that German tax increases will enhance the relative advantage of related diversifiers in Germany.27Indeed, unreported analysis at the firm-year level usingSales percentage in Germanyas the dependent variable and including firm effects and parent firm controls (size, public firm, product diversification, international diversification, GDP per capita, and GDP per capita growth) showed that the sales percentage that a firm generates in Germany decreased by 0.8 percentage points in the postreform period relative to the prereform period (p< 1%). 6.9. Firm Value Tied to Related Diversification It is possible the stock market recognizes the potential extra value creation opportunities through redeployment potential within related diversifiers. Two additional analyses are used to test this proposition. The first examination uses an event study methodology to exploit the stock market reaction to the approval of the reform by the German Bundesrat on July 7, 2006 and how shareholder reactions might be influenced by whether firms are relatedly diversified. The stock market reaction is captured by the cumulative abnormal return (CAR) during an “event window” computed by using the market model and portfolio approach (Brown and Warner 1985). Because the event occurs for all firms on the same day, we correct for cross-sectional correlation of abnormal returns. The sample, which consists of 17 public retail firms that are listed on the German stock exchange in 2006, and the calculation of CAR are described in detail in Online Appendix C. Table C1 in the Online Appendix reports the results. It shows that results for the portfolio of related diversified firms are significant on the event day (0) and over the two-day event windows including the event day (−1, 0) and (0, 1). For example, on the event day (i.e., July 7, 2006), the CAR is 4.4% for the portfolio of related diversifiers. The CARs of related diversified firms in the two-day intervals excluding the event day (i.e., (−2, −1) and (1, 2)) are substantively smaller and insignificant. Results are also insignificant during all event windows for a portfolio consisting of unrelated diversified and focused firms. These findings suggest that the approval of the reform by the Bundesrat had a positive influence on the portfolio return of related diversified firms but had no influence on the portfolio return of unrelated diversified and focused firms.28 A second examination considers a broader sample of both treated and control firms between 2003 and 2010 using panel data analysis. The sample is described in Online Appendix C. It includes 43 firms (17 treated firms that are listed on the German stock exchange and 26 control firms that are listed on stock exchanges in other EU countries) for the analysis of market-to-book values and 42 firms (17 treated firms and 25 control firms) for the analysis of excess returns. Table C2 in the Online Appendix reports the results usingMarket-to-book valueandExcess returnas the dependent variables.29Column (1) in Table C2 in the Online Appendix indicates that in the years following the 2006 German RETT reform, market-to-book values of retail firms listed on public exchange in Germany (these were all German firms) declined by 1.5%, on average, relative to retail firms listed on public exchanges in other EU countries (p< 1%). Results also reveal that the negative association between the RETT reform and the market-to-book values of German firms is attenuated for related diversified firms, as indicated by the significant and positive interaction effect between theGerman firms postreformandRelated diversified firmvariables (p< 1%). To elaborate, column (4) in Table C2 in the Online Appendix shows that following the tax reform, the market-to-book value of a German firm decreases by 1.6% if the firm is unrelatedly diversified or focused on a single business, whereas it decreases by only 0.6% if the firm is relatedly diversified relative to control firms in other EU countries. UsingExcess returnas the dependent variable produces substantively similar results (see columns (5)–(8) in Table C2 in the Online Appendix). These findings suggest that the stock market anticipated that the relative advantage of related diversifiers should increase following the 2006 German RETT reform. 6.10. Did Firms Redeploy Stores After 2006? The focus of this paper is on the probability of firms becoming related diversifiers to generate internal markets, where these firms have an option but not an obligation to redeploy in the future. Our analysis focused on the establishment of this option. Of course, having the option to redeploy does not imply that redeployment will occur. Neither does a lack of redeployment detract from the value of having the potential. Nevertheless, the proposed mechanism is further illuminated if postexpansion redeployment sometimes obtains within our sampled firms. Anecdotal evidence indicates that it does. For example, in 2007, German-based Do it yourself (DIY) retailer Globus acquired Hela in the same DIY format with 31 stores in Germany. During 2019 and 2020, Globus redeployed six Hela stores to Globus Baumarkt in a corporate restructuring process. Furthermore, in 2007, another German-based DIY retailer Praktiker acquired Max Bahr in the same DIY format with 77 stores in Germany. In 2012, Praktiker started a corporate restructuring program with the redeployment of 57 Praktiker stores to the more profitable sister chain Max Bahr until 2013. In 2013, the United States-based Foot Locker added in Germany the chain Runners Point. During 2020 and 2021, all 72 Runners Point stores were closed in Germany, and many of them were converted into Foot Locker stores (HahnAG 2021). Lastly, in 2020, the German-based shoe retailer Deichmann abandoned its midprice brand Roland in the same shoe store format including 45 stores after about 32 years of operation. By the end of 2019, 20 stores were transferred to the more profitable portfolio chain Görtz Schuhe, and they were quickly rebranded as Görtz (HahnAG 2020). Interestingly, firms redeployed both purchased and leased stores, with some retailers, like Praktiker, redeploying mainly leased units.30To complement these direct observations, we searched for large-sample evidence of store redeployment from exiting to surviving portfolio chains as found bySohl and Folta (2021). If store redeployment occurs and our theory around redeployment costs is correct, upon a chain’s country exit, we should observe more store growth in surviving related sibling chains than in surviving unrelated sibling chains. The 2008 global financial crisis (GFC) is used as an environmental shock—a period during which exit via internal redeployment likely became more pronounced because external markets were less efficient. Countries were hit by the GFC at different times, and the systemic banking crisis database (Laeven and Valencia 2013) was used to identify this timing. Unreported analysis of 1,663 chains in all 27 EU countries showed that if a focal country was hit by the GFC, a surviving chain’s store growth increased by 2.0 percentage points with each additional store of an exiting same-format sibling (p< 1%), conditional on chain-country effects and control variables. In contrast, the increase in store growth was only 0.2 percentage points in noncrisis times (p< 5%), suggesting that more firms exit via divestment (i.e., by selling stores in the external market) in more stable economic times. Moreover, results revealed that exits of different-format siblings were not significantly associated with a focal chain’s store growth, whether the chain’s country was hit by the GFC or not. These large-sample patterns solidify our view that, in our context, related diversification is a necessary condition for exercising the option to redeploy stores if conditions warrant.
article.7. Discussion: A spate of recent research clarifies how and when multibusiness firms might benefit from internal (noncapital) resource markets, and this research is beginning to provide empirical evidence on these issues. Prior to this paper, however, it has been unknown whether firms proactively diversify to generate these internal markets. We take advantage of an anticipated German real estate transfer tax increase, which would eventually raise the irreversibility of real estate investment, to provide evidence that retail firms move quickly to benefit from the corporate flexibility inherent in more efficient internal markets for real estate. Several theoretical and empirical contributions are worth elaborating. First, we speak to the dynamics of diversification. An evolving literature on resource redeployment has largely focused on benefits subsequent to diversification but has been relatively silent around whether these benefits inspire diversification or whether myopic managers merely redeploy within the portfolio they inherit. LikeLieberman et al. (2017), we draw attention to the fact that firms should be more inclined to pursue related diversification because lower irreversibility of investment lowers the required expected profit to induce entry. We clarify that this inclination should escalate with greater external market inefficiency. In this sense, we explain how diversification decisions are linked to the relative efficiency in internal versus external markets for nonfinancial resources. Second, we are able to focus on redeployability as an explanation for related diversification. This is surely because of our empirical context in the European retail market and choice of natural experiment. Because retail stores have the characteristics of a firm-flexible, nonscale-free resource (i.e., they are tradable in the open market and have opportunity cost in their usage), the threat of higher market exchange costs for retail stores and the exemption from such costs for internal reallocations should be largely tied to diversification decisions inspired by the potential for store redeployment. We find compelling evidence that rates of related diversification increase in Germany relative to other EU countries, and we interpret this as firms diversifying to create internal resource markets to substitute for anticipated decreases in external market efficiency. Adding to our confidence in this conclusion is that (a) several firms that became related diversifiers in response to the treatment exercised the option to redeploy stores, (b) the legislation does not inspire unrelated diversification, (c) firms focusing on franchising are less sensitive to the policy reform, (d) firms with larger real estate holdings are more likely to pursue related diversification, and (e) there is a strong relationship between actual property transfer tax increases and related diversification across the EU. Third, we have argued that both owning and leasing real estate should enable the potential to redeploy. We know of no other studies that consider whether redeployment potential is available through leasing. This is important because retail firms nearly always use a combination of both. Evidence is consistent with the view that firms with either high or low lease-intensive real estate portfolios are more likely to pursue related diversification if transfer taxes are expected to increase. The fact that more lease-intensive firms are less likely to pursue related diversification lends credence to the idea that compared with owning, leasing does not provide the same level of discretion to redeploy and impetus to diversify quickly. Because we do not observe to what extent related diversification occurs through leasing or owning, it may be possible that diversification through leasing is preferred to owning, but our analysis of retail firms suggests that lease ratios do not increase subsequent to reform implementation. Fourth, our work relates to prior research on internal capital markets. In particular, the theoretical analysis ofStein (1997)focuses on redeployment of financial resources, where the key advantage of the multibusiness firm is informational. Managers have unique insight into opportunities, and this insight is accentuated for related diversified firms. Our internal market story contains those elements, but it is accentuated when a firm’s nonfinancial resources lack market liquidity and can be internally redeployed at some cost. We believe that our natural experiment of the German RETT reform complements prior research on the allocation of financial resources because it has a direct bearing upon the liquidity of property, a key resource in retail. Finally, our research reconciles with but also extends a large literature in financial economics examining the impact of transfer taxes on transactions in the real estate market (e.g.,Dachis et al. 2012,Lieser and Groh 2014,Devaney et al. 2017,Best and Kleven 2018). This literature uniformly predicts that increases in transfer taxes will decrease investment in property. We show that this need not necessarily be obtained in the short term if the possibility of transfer taxes encourages the creation of an internal market for property as an alternative to the external market. By considering the paradox of higher transfer tax rates and more diversifying entry into business lines at the fine-grained level of submarket entry, our study provides novel insights into the real effects of transfer tax policies. Our paper has several limitations, which provide directions for future research. Given our large-scale empirical effort to test how the potential for redeployment affects related diversification, it is important to note that we do not empirically distinguish between the potential for resource sharing and resource redeployment as determinants of related diversification decisions, and we search for evidence consistent with our theoretical prediction. In particular, we try to overcome this by testing theoretical conditions and choosing a context where the dynamic benefits of relatedness are the most plausible explanation of decisions to combine related businesses into a portfolio. We do not directly observe redeployment beyond anecdotal evidence. Indeed, it is not our intent to observe redeployment as it exemplifies the exercise of the switching option and has been examined elsewhere (e.g.,Belenzon and Tsolmon 2016,Sohl and Folta 2021). Rather, our focus is on the initiation of such an option. A potential concern is generalizability. As with many interesting policy experiments, a question is whether the findings are informative beyond the 2006 German RETT reform as a source of market frictions and our focus on the potential for redeployment of real estate (stores) in the retail industry. AlthoughLieberman et al. (2017)expect that the potential for resource redeployment might inspire diversifying entry, their interviews suggest that managers do not consider flexibility when entering. We believe that our focus on the retail industry may be particularly appropriate for diagnosing farsightedness in anticipating redeployment because our interviews and anecdotal evidence suggest store redeployment is common for related diversified firms. One executive at Inditex suggests that redeployment occurs regularly. Although our findings should certainly apply to other service industries in which multiunit chains are a prevalent organizational form, we believe that the framework described in this paper is more generally applicable across various sources of market frictions and industry contexts. As just one example, firms in the airline industry often operate multiple businesses (i.e., airlines) in parallel. If buying and selling or licensing airplanes become more costly because of market frictions, it is possible that switching airplanes between portfolio businesses creates value if there are inducements net of redeployment cost to do so. It is also worth emphasizing that our dichotomous distinction between “related” and “unrelated” diversification is specific to our retail context, where store format is a fundamental determinant of redeployment costs. Other contexts may usher in more subtle increments of redeployment costs, requiring a less discrete treatment of types of diversification. Location and competition are certainly important issues in retail. We fully expect that competitive preemption plays an important role in decisions to create an internal market for retail stores to the extent that firms may prefer to acquire or hold onto valuable properties, both owned and leased, by having the option to internally redeploy them. Informal discussions with executives at Inditex suggest that this is a common occurrence. Future research might pursue this line of inquiry: for example, by examining how transfer taxes and considerations of competitive preemption might jointly affect firm scope decisions. Overall, by emphasizing the dynamic benefits of relatedness arising from resource redeployability, this paper contributes to an improved understanding of the influence of relatedness on diversification decisions. It, therefore, sheds new light on a fundamental question in strategy research of what value the corporate parent adds and the consequent implications for firm boundary choices (Rumelt et al. 1994).
